CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early, making it easy to follow the high-level narrative. Notation appears consistent and the methodological components (CNN for text, TransE-style encoder, margin losses) are described with sufficient detail; hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is thorough. Figures/tables and examples are adequate and informative for the reported experiments, though some sections read terse and could benefit from clearer signposting of assumptions and the interplay of loss terms. Overall writing is adequate with minor clarity issues but no major grammatical or jargon-overload problems. | B(4): Overall the paper is well-organized and follows a logical flow from problem motivation through model description to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated clearly up front (joint text+behavior embedding, TransE-style behavioral encoder, and cold-start focus). Notation and loss formulation appear consistent and sufficiently defined for reproducibility (hyperparameters are reported), and related work coverage is thorough. The writing is adequate with only minor presentation issues — figures/tables and examples are described but not highlighted as unusually strong, and code availability is not mentioned which would have improved practical clarity and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well-organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early, making it easy to follow the high-level narrative. Notation appears consistent and the methodological components (CNN for text, TransE-style encoder, margin losses) are described with sufficient detail; hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is thorough. Figures/tables and examples are adequate and informative for the reported experiments, though some sections read terse and could benefit from clearer signposting of assumptions and the interplay of loss terms. Overall writing is adequate with minor clarity issues but no major grammatical or jargon-overload problems.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well-organized and follows a logical flow from problem motivation through model description to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated clearly up front (joint text+behavior embedding, TransE-style behavioral encoder, and cold-start focus). Notation and loss formulation appear consistent and sufficiently defined for reproducibility (hyperparameters are reported), and related work coverage is thorough. The writing is adequate with only minor presentation issues — figures/tables and examples are described but not highlighted as unusually strong, and code availability is not mentioned which would have improved practical clarity and reproducibility.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper adapts established components (CNN text encoders and a TransE-style triple embedding) and combines them for review spam detection, which is a reasonable but conceptually incremental move rather than a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. The formulation (jointly embedding texts with global behavioral triples and ratings) is a useful application-specific integration that is clearly differentiated from prior SVM and standalone CNN baselines, but it largely repurposes known mechanisms rather than introducing novel theory or a fundamentally new encoder. From the summary, the technique appears to be a non-trivial engineering combination tailored to cold-start, so it would inform practitioners but would not likely surprise an expert as a breakthrough innovation. | B(3): From my practical-systems view this work offers a moderate novelty: it adapts known components (CNN text encoder + TransE-style triple embeddings and margin losses) into a joint model tailored for the review spam cold-start problem and demonstrates empirical benefits. The idea of fusing global behavioral embeddings with text to augment sparse users is useful for applications, but methodologically it mostly combines existing techniques rather than proposing a fundamentally new model or learning paradigm. The paper clearly differentiates itself by applying the TransE-like encoding to (product, reviewer, review) triples and targeting cold-start spam detection, which would inform practitioners although it is an incremental advance for experts.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper adapts established components (CNN text encoders and a TransE-style triple embedding) and combines them for review spam detection, which is a reasonable but conceptually incremental move rather than a fundamentally new modeling paradigm. The formulation (jointly embedding texts with global behavioral triples and ratings) is a useful application-specific integration that is clearly differentiated from prior SVM and standalone CNN baselines, but it largely repurposes known mechanisms rather than introducing novel theory or a fundamentally new encoder. From the summary, the technique appears to be a non-trivial engineering combination tailored to cold-start, so it would inform practitioners but would not likely surprise an expert as a breakthrough innovation.

reviewer_b.justification: From my practical-systems view this work offers a moderate novelty: it adapts known components (CNN text encoder + TransE-style triple embeddings and margin losses) into a joint model tailored for the review spam cold-start problem and demonstrates empirical benefits. The idea of fusing global behavioral embeddings with text to augment sparse users is useful for applications, but methodologically it mostly combines existing techniques rather than proposing a fundamentally new model or learning paradigm. The paper clearly differentiates itself by applying the TransE-like encoding to (product, reviewer, review) triples and targeting cold-start spam detection, which would inform practitioners although it is an incremental advance for experts.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is a good fit for NLP/ML venues, proposing a sensible joint text+behavior embedding and demonstrating empirical gains on realistic Yelp data. I give a 4 because the work is practically relevant and experimentally solid, but it is held back from a top score by limited theoretical motivation, a narrower-than-expected set of baselines and ablations, and lack of broader cross-domain validation or code release to maximize reproducibility and impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic—joint text+behavior embeddings for review spam detection—fits NLP/ML interests, and the model (CNN + TransE-style behavioral encoder) and evaluation on Yelp are broadly appropriate. However, from the summary I see limited theoretical motivation or guarantees addressing cold-start (important to my reviewer criteria), a narrowly chosen set of baselines (no recent graph/neural or strong transfer baselines), and no mention of the rigorous ablation/significance testing I expect; the claimed ‘unsupervised’ domain-adaptability also appears under-supported. Given these concerns the paper is borderline for a top-tier NLP/ML venue rather than a clear fit.

reviewer_b.justification: Good fit for a top NLP/ML venue: the paper proposes a novel joint text+behavior embedding (CNN + TransE-style encoder) that directly targets a practical cold-start problem in review spam detection and demonstrates empirical gains on realistic Yelp data with standard metrics. The methodology and evaluation (comparisons to text-only and behavioral baselines, hyperparameters reported) match community expectations, and the emphasis on unsupervised/large-scale applicability aligns with applied ML interests. Minor scope concerns remain since the work is highly application-focused within information quality and would benefit from released code or broader cross-domain experiments to maximize impact at a top-tier ML/NLP conference.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important practical problem and demonstrates clear empirical gains by jointly embedding text and behavior, but that the contribution is incremental rather than transformative. Concerns about limited evaluation (only Yelp), missing comparisons to recent neural/graph baselines, lack of theoretical justification, and limited analysis of domain transfer reduce broader impact. Given the practical usefulness but constrained novelty and evaluation, a moderate impact score is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a meaningful and practical problem—cold-start in review spam detection—and proposes a joint CNN (text) + TransE-style global behaviour embedding that empirically improves over text-only and classical feature baselines on Yelp. However, from the provided summary the advance appears primarily empirical and incremental: there is no theoretical justification or guarantees for why the joint embedding specifically resolves cold-start, comparisons omit more recent neural/graph-based SOTA baselines, and claims about unsupervised domain-adaptability lack transfer/error analysis. The work is likely to be of moderate impact: useful as an applied technique and to spur further study of joint text–behaviour representations, but not immediately transformative for the field without stronger theoretical grounding, broader baselines, and public code for adoption.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important, practical problem—cold-start review spam detection—by proposing a joint text + behavioral embedding (CNN + TransE-style encoder) that appears to improve detection and domain adaptability on realistic Yelp data. From a systems perspective the method is promising because it augments sparse user behavior for new reviewers, is reported to work in an unsupervised/large-scale setting, and beats both text-only CNN and traditional behavioral-feature SVM baselines. It is unlikely to be purely transformative (novelty is incremental and evaluation is limited to Yelp), but the clear practical gains and scalability make it a strong contribution that practitioners and researchers will likely build on.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the experimental comparison is reasonably fair—same dataset, sensible baselines, and common metrics—but that important recent neural/graph-based or graph-embedding competitors are missing and there is no mention of statistical testing or released code. Because the evaluation is adequate but incomplete for strong claims about cold-start and domain adaptability, a middling score reflects fair but not fully comprehensive or rigorous comparison.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates against a set of sensible baselines (SVM with bigrams, multiple behavioral SVM variants, and an independent CNN) on the same Yelp dataset with common metrics, so basic fairness is preserved. However, from the summary it omits several strong recent and graph-based/state-of-the-art competitors (e.g., contemporary neural graph or ensemble approaches and explicit graph-embedding baselines), and I see no evidence of statistical significance testing or ablations isolating core components versus those competitors. The related-work discussion is reported as thorough, but empirical comparisons therefore remain incomplete relative to the expectations for rigorous claims about cold-start and domain adaptability.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates on a realistic Yelp dataset and compares to reasonable baselines (SVM with bigrams, SVMs with multiple behavioral feature sets, and an independent CNN), and uses consistent metrics (Precision/Recall/F1/Accuracy), so comparisons appear fair. However, from the provided summary it seems to omit newer neural or graph-based baselines that also combine text and behavior, and code/releases aren’t mentioned to aid reproducibility. The authors do discuss when behavioral features fail (cold-start) and analyze differences between their joint model and text-only/feature-based methods, but the omission of some recent competing approaches prevents a fully comprehensive comparison.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the experimental setup and baselines are reasonable and that the paper thoroughly discusses related work and limitations, but they also agree key controlled ablations, transfer/large-scale experiments, and full reproducibility artifacts are missing. Given these shared weaknesses that weaken support for the main mechanistic and scalability claims, a conservative score of 3 is appropriate while recognizing the work’s promising contributions.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental suite is adequate but incomplete: they evaluate on a standard Yelp split against text- and feature-based SVMs plus an independent CNN, and report standard metrics, but claims about domain-adaptability and unsupervised large-scale applicability are not supported by explicit transfer experiments or large-scale ablation. Ablation/sensitivity analyses appear insufficient or under-specified (no mention of ≥3 controlled ablations isolating the TransE-like encoder, rating-constraint loss, and joint training), which my persona penalizes given the core mechanistic claim about augmenting sparse behavior. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, and limitations about behavioral-feature failure modes are discussed, but reproducibility is only partially satisfied (hyperparameters given but no code release mentioned). Overall the work is promising but misses critical controlled analyses and stronger baselines to fully support its main claims.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the experimental setup is solid: evaluation on the Yelp spam dataset with sensible baselines (text-only CNN, SVMs with linguistic and behavioral features), standard metrics, and reported hyperparameters supports the main claims that joint text+behavior embeddings help in cold-start. The paper also discusses limitations of behavioral features and claims unsupervised scalability, and related work coverage is thorough. However, the summary does not mention clear ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., isolating the TransE-like component, varying sparsity levels beyond Yelp) or publicly available code/detailed reproducibility artifacts, leaving some practical and generalization questions unanswered.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes clear, plausible claims and provides empirical evidence that joint text+behavior embeddings improve spam detection, and that the mathematical formulation is standard and internally consistent. However, they also agree the evaluation lacks sufficient rigor — missing key ablations, comparisons to recent SOTA/graph-based methods, statistical significance testing, and transfer experiments or code release — which weakens the strength of the technical claims. Given these methodological gaps, a moderate score reflecting likely correctness but limited empirical validation is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and provides empirical evidence that a joint CNN+TransE-style model improves detection over text-only and traditional behavioral baselines on Yelp, but the experimental methodology is insufficiently rigorous for strong technical validation. Key deficiencies: fewer than three targeted ablations isolating core components are reported, comparisons omit at least two recent neural/graph-based state-of-the-art alternatives, and no statistical significance testing is described. The mathematical formulation appears standard (TransE-like margin losses) and likely correct, but claims about unsupervised applicability and domain adaptability are not supported by explicit transfer experiments or error analysis, leaving some logical gaps and limiting how strongly the conclusions are justified.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear claims and supports them with experiments showing joint text+behavior embeddings outperform text-only and traditional feature baselines on the Yelp spam datasets, and reports hyperparameters and multiple baselines (CNN, SVM variants). The mathematical component is straightforward (TransE-like encoder plus margin losses) and appears internally consistent, though no formal proofs are required. Methodologically it is generally sound — relevant baselines and ablations on sparsity are included — but the summary omits details like statistical significance tests, code release, and evidence for the claimed cross-domain adaptability beyond the reported dataset, which are practical reproducibility and generalization concerns. Overall the technical claims are plausible and empirically supported, with only minor gaps in reported evaluation rigor and reproducibility.

main_claims: Proposes a novel neural network model to handle the cold-start problem in review spam detection by jointly embedding textual and behavioral information.
Shows that jointly embedding review texts with global behavioral information (via a TransE-like encoder) improves detection performance compared to text-only and traditional feature based baselines.

Demonstrates the model augments sparse behavioral information for new reviewers and achieves effective performance and preferable domain-adaptability, and can be applied to large-scale data in an unsupervised way.
_summary.methodology: Joint model that learns review text embeddings via a CNN and encodes global behavioral information using a TransE-style embedding of (product, reviewer, review) triples, plus jointly learned rating embeddings; trained with margin-based losses combining the TransE loss and a rating-constraint loss.
datasets: Yelp dataset (as used in Mukherjee et al., 2013b,c; Rayana and Akoglu, 2015)
baselines: SVM with behavioral features (RL, RD, MCS)
SVM with behavioral features formed from abundant information
Independent supervised CNN (same structure as CNN part)
limitations: Traditional statistical behavioral features are ineffective in the cold-start scenario due to extremely sparse user behavior.
Behavioral features can cause increased false positives (decrease in F1 and Recall) when behavioral information is poor.
Behavioral features only work well when abundant behavioral information is available (i.e., after users post many reviews).

reviewer_a.name: Theoretical Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in machine learning with a focus on representation learning theory and formal evaluation of models in NLP and information retrieval.
traits: Penalizes papers that present empirical improvements without formal motivation or theoretical analysis (will deduct points if no explanation/guarantee for why proposed joint embedding should address cold-start).
Requires rigorous ablation and statistical significance testing (will downgrade if fewer than 3 ablations isolating core components and no significance tests reported).
Insists on strong, well-matched baselines including recent neural and graph-based methods (will subtract points if comparisons omit at least 2 state-of-the-art alternatives).

Skeptical of claims about 'unsupervised' applicability and domain-adaptability without explicit transfer experiments and error analysis (will penalize if transfer/domain claims are anecdotal or limited).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I focus on mechanistic explanation, theory-grounded motivation, and comprehensive controlled experiments; I reward clear causal or theoretical justification and penalize under-specified empirical claims.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Systems Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist in applied NLP and information quality, with experience building large-scale spam and abuse detection systems deployed in production.
traits: Favors papers that show clear empirical gains on realistic, large-scale datasets and will reward demonstrated improvements in practical metrics (gives credit for wall-clock scalability and unsupervised/weakly-supervised methods).
Prefers methods that jointly leverage multiple signal types (text + behavior) and will upscore novel, well-engineered multi-modal embeddings that improve cold-start performance in practice.

Values reproducibility and engineering practicality: rewards when code, data splits, and training details are provided and when methods are efficient at scale (will downgrade if training/inference complexity is prohibitively high or not reported).

Accepts fewer theoretical guarantees if the system demonstrates robust, consistent gains across domains and realistic sparsity settings (will be generous when cross-domain/adaptation experiments and ablations on sparsity are thorough).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I prioritize empirical impact, system scalability, and applicability to real-world problems; I reward methods that convincingly improve deployed-use cases even if theory is light.
